questionText: Solve the equation $\sin\theta-\cos\theta=1$ for $0\le\theta\le 4\pi$, giving your answers as exact multiples of $\pi$. Use the form $R\sin(\theta-\alpha)$ with $R>0$ and $0<\alpha<\dfrac{\pi}{2}$.

steps.explanation: The cosine term is subtracted, so we match against $R\sin(\theta-\alpha)$, whose expansion carries the same minus sign in front of $\cos\theta$.
Expanding the compound angle gives a $\sin\theta$ term minus a $\cos\theta$ term, ready to compare with the left-hand side.
Matching the coefficients of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$ gives two equal equations because both coefficients on the left are $1$.
Squaring and adding eliminates $\alpha$ to give $R^2=2$, so the amplitude is the exact surd $\sqrt{2}$.
Dividing the equal equations gives $\tan\alpha=1$; working in radians, the acute angle is $\alpha=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.
Substituting the single wave and dividing by $\sqrt{2}$ gives a standard sine value $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2}$ whose solutions are exact.

Because we solve for $\theta-\dfrac{\pi}{4}$, we shift the interval down by $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$. The upper limit becomes $4\pi-\dfrac{\pi}{4}=\dfrac{15\pi}{4}$, and the wide interval means several solutions.
The principal solution of $\sin x=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt2}$ is $x=\dfrac{\pi}{4}$.
Sine is positive in the second quadrant, giving $\pi-\dfrac{\pi}{4}=\dfrac{3\pi}{4}$.

steps.description: Add a full turn to each
steps.explanation: Sine repeats every $2\pi$, so adding $2\pi=\dfrac{8\pi}{4}$ to each base value gives $\dfrac{9\pi}{4}$ and $\dfrac{11\pi}{4}$, both still inside the shifted interval.
A second full turn pushes the angle past the upper limit $\dfrac{15\pi}{4}$, so there are no more solutions to include.
These are all four values of the shifted angle that lie in $\left[-\dfrac{\pi}{4},\dfrac{15\pi}{4}\right]$.
Adding $\dfrac{\pi}{4}$ back to each shifted value gives the four values of $\theta$ as exact multiples of $\pi$.
Checking $\theta=\pi$ gives $0-(-1)=1$, confirming the equation holds; all four values lie within $0\le\theta\le4\pi$.
